name: Policy Impact Framework
summary: A structured approach to assessing whether a policy proposal will achieve its intended outcomes — covering economic, social, environmental, and political impacts.
whenToUseIt: When evaluating any proposed policy change, when writing a policy brief for a minister, when preparing for a parliamentary debate, or when comparing competing policy options.
steps: Define the problem clearly — what specific outcome is the policy trying to achieve?
Identify the target population — who is this policy meant to help?
Assess economic impact — costs to government, costs to business, costs to individuals, economic growth effects
Assess social impact — equity, access, community cohesion, vulnerable populations
Assess environmental impact — emissions, biodiversity, resource use, long-term sustainability
Assess political impact — public opinion, electorate effects, party dynamics, media framing
Identify second-order effects — what unintended consequences might emerge?
Define success metrics — how will we know if this policy is working in 1, 3, and 5 years?
example: Applying the framework to a proposed increase in the minimum wage: Problem — working poverty. Target — low-income workers. Economic impact — higher wages for 2.6 million workers, potential job losses in some sectors, increased consumer spending. Social impact — reduced inequality, improved health outcomes for low-income families. Environmental impact — negligible. Political impact — popular with Labor/Greens voters, opposed by business groups, complex in marginal seats with many small businesses. Second-order effects — potential price increases passed to consumers, automation accelerating in some industries. Success metrics — real wages growth, employment rate, poverty rate after 2 years.
